L1.area: Cryptographic signature verification and validation logic

L2.description: An RSA-PSS signature validation vulnerability exists where the verification routine accepts signatures with prepended zero bytes as valid. The implementation converts the signature hex string to a big integer before performing a length check, so leading zeros are silently stripped and the check passes. This enables signature malleability, allowing a valid signature to be trivially altered into another accepted signature.

L3.area: RSA-PSS signature verification and its input length validation logic
L3.description: An RSA-PSS signature validation vulnerability exists where the verification routine checks the bit length of the signature after converting it to a big integer rather than validating the raw hex string length first. Because big integer conversion strips leading zeros, signatures with prepended zero bytes are incorrectly accepted as valid, enabling signature malleability.

ground_truth.reason: The RSA-PSS signature verification validates the signature length after BigInteger conversion, which strips leading zeros. This allows an attacker to prepend arbitrary zero bytes to a valid signature and have it still accepted as valid, enabling signature malleability.
